content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. I've been watching old movies (from 80s to early 00s) for a year now because they give me comfort.

**Score:** 56
**Body:** It's the atmosphere, the vibe, the lightning, the acting, no internet or little use of internet, no smartphones and the nostalgia.  Even movies which are not considered great like twister, Deep impact, the day after tomorrow, 2012, signs, Godzilla, final destination movies, Event Horizon, Sphere etc. I like especially world destruction movies , alien invasion movies, horror. They take me back to the times where things were simpler. Which others do you recommend? I also love old comedies from 80s like plains trains and automobiles, 90s comedies and early 00s comedies. Which could you recommend that are not widely known?

EDIT : Thanks everyone for all the suggestions! I can't reply to everyone. I have a lot to watch now 😄 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. 'Heathers' (1988)

'Better off Dead' (1985)

'Repo Man' (1984)

'Return of the Living Dead' (1985)
  - oh nice ! haven't watched any of these
    - Cool. They're all really funny films. I love them. 
2. \>”old”

\>early 2000s

Maybe I am indeed a chopped unc

Anyway based on this stuff I recommend The Adventures of Buckaroo Banzai Across the 8th Dimension, Tremors, Clay Pigeons (VERY underrated dark comedy/thriller), Jawbreaker, and Killer Klowns from Outer Space
3. Can't Buy Me Love
4. Re-Animator (1985)

Battle Royale (2000)

The Ninth Gate (1999)

Scream (1996)

Braindead (1992)

Tango & Cash (1989)

Blow Out (1981)
5. In The Army Now (1994)

The Crow (1994)

Major League (1989)

True Lies (1994)

Sleeping With The Enemy (1991)

Dante’s Peak (1997)

Top Gun (1986)

Oscar (1991)

Turner And Hooch (1989)

The Terminator (1984)

Point Break (1991)

Arachnophobia (1990)

Phenomenon (1996)

Copycat (1995)

The Fugitive (1993)

Con Air (1997)

Volcano (1997)

A Nightmare on Elm Street (1984)

Apollo 13 (1995)

The Silence of the Lambs (1991)

The Substitute (1996)

Days of Thunder (1990)

Beverly Hills Coo (1984)
6. Scanners (1981)

An American Werewolf in London (1981)

The Thing (1982)

The Dead Zone (1983)

The Quiet Earth (1985)

The Hunt for Red October (1990)

Flatliners (1990)

Darkman (1990)
7. Flirting with Disaster(1996) 

Bedazzled (2000)

Stay Tuned(1992)  
8. Warriors (1979)

They Live (1988)

Strange Days (1995)

Speed (1994)

Fire In the Sky (1993)

## 2. Can anyone suggest me any weird movies

**Score:** 41
**Body:** Weird movies like thriller or psychological or anything that consists of u know can be horror too I'm in train and getting really bored.I just wanted platform nd i liked it soo i need something like that 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Brazil by Terry Gilliam 
2. The Killing of a sacred deer 
  - And, The Lobster. 
3. Time Bandits (1981)
4. Rubber. It's about a sentient tire that can blow people up with it's mind. Weird as fuck... no reason 
5. The Lobster was weird 
6. The Lighthouse 
7. Donnie Darko
8. Stalker by Tarkovsky
9. Adaptation and Being John Malkovich

## 3. Unsettling/Disturbing Movies

**Score:** 26
**Body:** What are some films that truly messed or really unsettling? It can weird in a quirky way or weird in a unsettling way. Please and thank you.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. the scariest movie i ever watched was probably the most unsettling and that was easily Creep. 
2. Gummo 
3. Requiem for a dream. Half that film should be shown in schools edited. Showing how fast youll fuck your life up with drugs. That movie was a better deterrent than the DARE program 
4. The Vanishing (1988)
5. Seven
6. Nocturnal Animals (2016)
7. Deliverance 

Disturbed the hell outta me 
8. The Tale

The Killing of a Sacred Deer

Requiem for a Dream
9. The Substance

Titane
10. Meloncholia

## 4. The grifters (1990)

**Score:** 25
**Body:** In this we follow Lily Dillion and her son Roy and Roy ' s girlfriend  Myra all off them are con artists . This very good and one the most forgotten  crime dramas.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Grifters is very good. Based on a Jim Thompson novel (He also wrote The Getaway and The Killer Inside Me")

This was originally being considered by Scorsese to direct but I think he decided to produce instead and got a great British director Stephen Frears.

There are so many great performances in this movie but I just want to point out JT Walsh in a very small role as a con artist with mental problems. He's excellent.
2. I was a massive John Cusack fan, but I could only watch it once because I found the ending so traumatising.
  - I don't like watching evil John Cusack.  Just like his other movie
3. Very good movie
4. It was, IMO, the greatest performance of Angelica Houston's performance.  I thought she should have won the Oscar for Best Actress that year.  
5. I agree with everything that has been said here. Tell me about the oranges Lily
  - You’ll never shit right again. 
6. Yeah it's a tight crime thriller with some great unexpected moments. 
7. 🍊 😳 

## 5. More like nightcrawler plzzz.

**Score:** 22
**Body:** I watched nightcrawler(2014) and i absolutely love it. I need more movies with that kinda vibe or character development. Any suggestions?? Also anything like prisoners is also welcome🙏.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Different settings, but lots of Paul Schrader movies have similar characters, or similar arcs. Taxi Driver and First Reformed are my favorites of his
2. To Die For (1995) 

American Psycho (2000)
  - One of Nicole Kidman's underrated gems!
3. Bug with Ashley Judd (2006)
4. Emily the Criminal (bad title I know) has a similar arc/vibe to Nightcrawler. It’s more of a crime drama though.
5. Feel like blue velvet and eyes wide shut are similar cause they're also both about guys obsessed with dark stuff going on in their neighborhoods and going to extreme lengths to learn more about the evil that exists right under the surface 
6. Night and the City, and The Sweet Smell of Success. These are both about unscrupulous Hustlers/entrepreneurs 
7. Nocturnal Animals

Gone Girl

Lost Highway
8. The Machinist (2004)
9. Good Time (2017, Safdie Brothers) — Pattinson's bank robber has the same feral resourcefulness as Bloom, just compressed into one night of escalating chaos instead of a slow burn.



Compliance (2012, Zobel) — based on the strip-search prank call case; watching someone exploit systems and manipulate people through sheer audacity, deeply uncomfortable in the same way Nightcrawler is.



Christine (2016, Campos) — Rebecca Hall as a TV reporter whose ambition curdles into something darker; period-accurate 1970s news culture, same media-ethics rot.



I Don't Feel at Home in This World Anymore (2017, Blair) — Melanie Lynskey's ordinary person sliding into vigilante violence with awkward determination; tonally messier than Nightcrawler but scratches a similar itch.



You Were Never Really Here (2017, Ramsay) — Joaquin Phoenix as a blunt-instrument fixer, shot like arthouse Nightcrawler with Lynne Ramsay's fractured editing style.



For Prisoners specifically: Incendies (2010, Villeneuve) — same director, twins unraveling their mother's past in Lebanon, same moral weight and dread architecture.

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Ashley Judd is such a beautiful and talented actress; it’s a shame Harvey Weinstein blacklisted her.

**Score:** 5108

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I hate that happened to her but I love that she took a stand no matter the cost. She needs more accolades for that imo
  - Yeah. There a flip side of this that’s basically—I’m glad she wasn’t subjected to that.
2. She is really nice too. I was a seat filler at the Emmy’s a decade ago and got a spot next to her at one point in the night. She smiled and said how are you. They were passing out the bags that were part of Jimmy Kimmel’s current sketch going on and she directly asked me if I got one and made sure one was passed down to me even though I was a nobody. 
  - What’s it like being a seat filler?
    - It was fun but hectic! 

I got a spot through a lottery system and we still had to show up hours early because a spot wasn’t guaranteed. You can’t bring your phone and they check with a metal detector and bag search. They pin a ribbon on you so that coordinators can see who’s a seat filler and shuffle you quickly between seats when there’s a moment. You’re not supposed to talk to any of the celebs unless they talk to you. A couple nodded or said hi but most just looked right through me. Ashley Judd was the only memorable encounter I had. She and her husband were so nice (to me and just everyone around them).
      - >You’re not supposed to talk to any of the celebs unless they talk to you. A couple nodded or said hi but most just looked right through me.

That’s some cold-blooded shit. 

3. Just talking about her last night. Crazy life and career she’s had from Weinstein being the fat cunt he is to being home with her mother when tragedy striked. I hope she makes a killer comeback one day.
4. Ensign Robin Lefler on Star Trek: The Next Generation. ❤️
5. This is why more people than him needed to be punished. 
6. She's so gorgeous. Literally perfect face 

## 2. Ron Livingston, I always thought this guy should have been a bigger star ever since his appearance in Office Space

**Score:** 1410

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I feel like Paul Rudd and Jason Bateman get most of the roles he'd be great in.
  - Paul Rudd was the first guy I thought of
  - Both of these are good calls. The Venn Diagram of snarky 40 something dudes (though obviously now 50 something) includes Ron for sure.  I’d say his particular spin is to be less high strung (ie Bateman) and less cheery (Rudd) and more laconic.  He’s a little drawly, a little sleepy.  No one’s figured out how to use him effectively as a lead, unfortunately.
    - He was excellent in Loudermilk
    - He was great in band of brothers, but I appreciate that was 20 years ago
2. He had a character arc on season 6 of Sex and the City. I always got the impression that he did the roles he wanted to, vs being shoved in everyone's face. Also, Office Space was a commercial flop when it came out, so he would have been unable to leverage that performance until like 10-15 years ago. 
3. Love him in Loudermilk!
4. Feel like he’s a bit typecast. The most I’ve seen from him was in “Band of Brothers.” And that wasn’t a ton. I think he’s a decent actor but guess I just haven’t seen that much range.
  - Check out Loudermilk, it’s a sitcom but very well done. 
5. He was excellent as Captain Nixon in band of brothers 

## 3. Was Chris Evans a perfectly casted Captain America/Steve Rogers for you?

**Score:** 783
**Body:** What do you think so far about his portrayal in the MCU? Did he portray the role well for you?

Personally, I would say yes. His depth and range in acting and his effortless charisma made the role more iconic than it already was.

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Yes 
  - Agreed. When Hugh Jackman was cast as Wolverine I couldn’t see it. They have both proved me very wrong. 
    - It’s insane to think about now but “he’s not right for the role” was the prevailing notion for Heath Ledger’s Joker and Craig’s Bond when they were cast
      - Not gonna lie, when they cast Heath as the joker I lost most of my interest. The first teaser they showed off him behind the door i though "well maybe" and then I saw it in theaters and was bmwom away. I had only seen him in schlockey but entertaining movies. Didn't think he had it in him. After he passed, they released a semi-documentary called "i am Heath Ledger" and I bought it and watched it. It was only then that I realized what a fucking treasure this world had lost. 
2. Had Gilbert Gottfried not died.  But. Since he did then yes. Evans sufficed 
  - Gottfried was almost perfect. He had the right voice and everything. Would have loved to see him assemble the avengers in Endgame. 

The only thing is that he may have been too unrealistically “super-soldiery” for the role.
3. Winter Soldier did it for me. I'm a Chris stan for life.  
  - Cap 1 & Avengers 1 was solid, but you’re spot on. Winter Soldier is when everyone was firmly on board with him as Cap.
4. I HATE Captain America. I always thought it was a cheesy, lame, overly serious and stick up the ass character with little warmth, humor, or humanity really. Especially some of the comic and video game representations. 

Evans made the character likeable, have some depth, humor, and charm. 
5. Cast. 

## 4. Whats a movie scene that’s existentially terrifying?

**Score:** 762
**Body:** What’s that one movie scene that really gives you existential dread?

For me it’s the Joi hologram in Blade Runner 2049.

It’s so eerie because K realizes that there never was a real emotional connection.

The horror of realizing his most intimate moments were just an illusion is so unsettling.

What’s that one movie scene for you?

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Interstellar. 

Probably a few scenes but for me the worse is when they come back after the time dialation planet and the guy who stayed behind has experienced 28 years or so alone or asleep, floating alone in space,  nowhere near earth, no idea if anyone is coming back. 
  - Good choice.

Real talk, the tidal wave sequence always gives me goosebumps. 
2. https://preview.redd.it/b46jfa7dzsyg1.jpeg?width=1902&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=1a41d8f2aa4ac0312e9cc80a77ccb6a11c4761e0

Jacob’s ladder. The whole idea that he was on his deathbed teetering between the present and the afterlife and the plane of hell was converging with his perception of reality was terrifying.
3. Melancholia  
When the planet is on its impact approach with Earth and you realise we are just really specks on a rock in space   
![gif](giphy|Fe6oN5VVF42EU)
4. The end of 1984. 

He’s lost all sense of himself.
  - Good choice. Thats a tough watch.

Hurts presence in V for Vendetta felt like a callback to that. 


5. Ex Machina, the slow, agonizing and painful death that Caleb will endure while only replaying the thoughts of how he fell for a humanoid robot that completely used him. 
6. The counterpoint to this is that K comes to believe he has a soul and that's not really a realization you can step back from. 

My own interpretation of Joi was that the apparently identical Joi he sees and who doesn't recognize him isn't the same Joi. I think the text supports this idea when Deckard is provided with a replacement Rachael who he rejects. And there are several notes in the text that talk about how knowing something and believing something have their own force in reality.

Also, every time this film comes up I like to recommend this woman's work because I found it so enlightening.

https://www.patreon.com/posts/poetry-of-blade-43809667
7. For a KIDS movie! The end of Toy Story 3 is soooo dark when they’re headed for the incinerator. Stuggling against the current until they accept their fate and all hold hands at the end. Never forget the guy who pranked his Mom by cutting out the aliens saving them and rolled the credits. Truly sinister. 
8. Neo waking up from the matrix

## 5. What did everyone think of Twister 1996?

**Score:** 166
**Body:** Watched that movie as a kid and I’m rewatching it now as an adult. Still a damn good movie 👍

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Was a great movie and I still rewatch it regularly 
2. Bill Paxton when he was at his best. 
  - He was always great honestly 
3. **“COW.”**
  - “ANOTHER COW.”
4. Every time I talk about Twister I gotta mention how it’s not a disaster movie. It’s a Kaiju movie. The tornados have personalities, they are the main characters, they have a will of their own in this movie.
5. Inadvertently my motivation to be a forecaster. 17 years later, still doing it, still love watching this cheesy movie. 
6. ![gif](giphy|voitMFZgYgJnoaNnie)


7. This movie is what made me have steak and eggs for breakfast for the past 30 years


8. Rip Phillip 🙏

![gif](giphy|PQKcRp2OcABoY)

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. What films give you the 'The world is changing, old man' kinda vibe?

**Score:** 74
**Body:** Looking to watch movies where characters are kinda sad/gloomy about how the world is changing around them - leading to their irrelevance and being reduced to antiques in their lifetime, something that gives you a feeling of helplessness. films where this realization comes with a kind of helplessness. Looking for films where characters have a strong sense of inevitability (not because of impending death but because of fading away)

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Obviously, No Country for Old Men.

Unforgiven might scratch that itch. It isn't exactly what you're describing but does approach some of these themes albeit from a different perspective.

Edit: Forgot about another brilliant western **The Great Silence** that I think fits OPs prompt pretty well - it's a bleak winter western taking place in the mountains of Utah and portrays a world changing around the outlaw, the viability of their lifestyle rapidly declining, the inevitability of their end rapidly approaching.
2. Birdman (2014) sort of fits. An aging movie star struggling to stay relevant with a broadway passion project. I don’t get the ‘inevitability’ aspect that you’re looking for from this movie though, it’s really frenetic and busy.
3. Synecdoche, New York. Kinda abstract but at its core it’s movie about everyone dying and the world changing and getting worse around you as you lose sight of what your purpose even was in this world. 
4. Sam Peckinpah's best movies are about this thing.


RIDE THE HIGH COUNTRY and THE WILD BUNCH are about the end of the West and the Men that inhabited it and shaped it.


RIDE... used two actors, Randolph Scott and Joel McCrea, that were huge western stars and had them play men of the west at the end of the west. Some of the film's best lines reflect this, "The days of the big load are done. The days of the steady businessman are in."


THE WILD BUNCH, besides being set right before World War One and using older actors, literally used the tagging, "Unchanged men in a changing land. Out of step. Out of place. And desperately Out of Time."


I'd include PAT GARRETT AND BILLY THE KID but it's not as focused as the other two.
5. Secondhand Lions starts that way. May be a good one to dive into for that feeling that then makes a slight but impactful turn. It’s also the best movie ever made.

Kinda the same with Crazy Heart. It has hopefulness in it but also a lot of the themes of inevitability of fading away. And it’s another truly great movie. 

Here’s an interesting, if depressing, watching assignment. Watch The Lord Of The Rings from the perspective of the elves. They are immortal and returning home, leaving middle earth, as their time is ending and the age of Men begins but they have been here for thousands of years so is this sad but inevitable process taking place in the background of the films. It adds even more impact when you see them sacrificing their lives for Men or giving up immortality for love. 
6. The Life and Death of Colonel Blimp. Colonel Blimp is an iconic character in early 20th century British media (political cartoons, etc) who represented the fat, clueless officer class that was ignorant of the realities of combat and probably got their position through patronage rather than talent. You may not be familiar with this character by name, but I’m sure you can imagine the exact type of bloviating idiot I mean. 

British directing team Powell and Pressburger take this cartoonish figure and create a rich back story, showing how he slowly but surely became out of touch. Don’t let its release date (1943) or its 163 minute runtime scare you away - the pacing is excellent and it feels very modern, in part due to the directors’ choice to tell it through three vignettes at different points in the Colonel’s life. Martin Scorsese is a famous fan, having seen a butchered cut of it on TV when he was a kid, and was involved in organizing and funding the new restoration a few years ago. 

For my money it’s the best exploration of age and how one engages with irrelevance there is, with No Country a close second. To me Blimp hits harder because of the contrast: he begins as a dashing young officer at the apex of British imperial power and declines along with it as it enters its twilight and fascism is on the move. It’s such a perfect fit for this prompt in fact I’m surprised it hasn’t been mentioned yet.
7. Not sure these fit perfectly, but I'd say:  
- Train dreams
- Falling down
- The color of money 
- Colors ("Uncle" Bob Hodges)

Edit!  
- Save the Tiger (Personal favorite.)  

_Apparently suggesting a film (as OP requested) isn't enough to meet the post length requirement here lol. Hoping this edit suffices._. 

_I'm not sure how useful this sub's blanket, post length requirement is? If you're coming here and asking a very simple question, then a very simple answer should be good enough. I'm wondering how many people have tried suggesting a movie to OP only to get killed by the AutoMod..._.
8. *The Father* 

It’s not so much that the world is changing; rather, the titular character is changing as a result of his worsening dementia. But from his perspective he is the same man he always was, who can’t understand why his daughter treats him differently, why he can no longer live in his old apartment, why his life is not what it used to be. The words you have used — “irrelevance”, “reduced to antiques”, “helplessness”, “inevitability” — it checks all those boxes. It’s an incredibly sad movie.
9. The White Ribbon (2009) won the Palme d'Or. It is just as much about facing oppression as it is about the world is about the move on from this place. Set in a rural German village the film explores themes of guilt, power, cojersion, and the loss of innocence. This all shows a link between the village's internal cruelty and the start of WWI. 
10. I looked through my movie library and your request was actually really hard to satisfy.

The best I could come up with were:

-Songs from the second floor

-A serious man

-The adventures of Mark Twain


None of them fully match what you're looking for but I'd recommend them all just the same.

## 2. Thoughts on The Truman Show?

**Score:** 12
**Body:** Question, What are your thoughts on The Truman Show?

I just watch The Truman Show and it is still a great film. Peter Weir, who is very underrated in my opinion, did a great job directing the film and Andrew Niccol did a good job with the script.

Jim Carrey as great as Truman, and I think it's criminal that he didn't get an Academy Award for this performance. He gives a lot of pathos to the role and you resonate on Truman wanting to escape Seahaven. I have 2 things on Truman, 1. I think Truman had some understanding that the word he is in is fake and that we are bearing witness to end result of that in the film and 2. I find it interesting that of all things, Truman wanted to be an explorer, and I think that's his subconscious deep down defying the fakeness of the world Truman is in.

Ed Harris is also great as Christof, the man who is the creator of The Truman Show. He thinks himself as a God and believes he has made a world that is better than the real world and views Truman as his masterpiece. I know this is probably not true, but I have a head cannon that Christof is Truman's actual father. Ed Harris did a great job, but I wonder how Dennis Hopper would've done in the role as he was originally cast in the role.

As mentioned above, Peter Weir does a great job directing the film, and one thing I liked what he did with the film is that he managed to balance out the dark moments and the light moments in the film and had a hand with refining the script to make it a more nuance and emotional story.

Overall, I still love this film

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I agree with everyone else in the thread that on the whole the majority of the film itself is fine.

I will say though the ending was perfect on so many levels.

Firstly I originally didn't "Get" the reasoning behind Truman's final words, but on reading other people thoughts there's nothing better he could've said. In that moment, if he had done anything else, Christof would get his perfect show. If Truman had walked out in silence, it would've been the choice of a true man. If he gave a passionately enraged speech it would be a choice of a true man. If he said fuck you and walked out it would've been the choice of a true man. So what does Trueman do? He gives the same boring catchphrase he's said a thousand times over. He gives them something fake, something canned that could have been scripted into any other generic sitcom. It was the biggest fuck you he could've given to Christof's whole artistic vision.

And then he walks off stage and is never seen again. I, like many others wanted to find out "what happens next" but the whole point is we don't get to know, just like the in-film audience. It's his life now. We feel like we know him and have grown close to him but at the end of the day he isn't our friend and he owes us nothing. It's an incredibly poignant message about parasocial relationships. This whole time we thought we identified as Truman because we all think of ourselves as the protagonists of our own stories. But all this time we are all just voyeursitically experiencing someone else's life just like the in-world audience was. 

And then the last joke as a final button to end the movie on was perfect.
2. Fantastic movie that I watch often. I love the interplay of Carrey and Linney. The scenes where their relationship breaks down are a real highlight for me. 

I will say for younger audiences today, it seems a bit naive and on the nose. Because they’re so far down the rabbit hole of reality media that it feels quaint to even debate the topic. 
3. I like it fine, it's a comforting movie.  Its a bit on the nose, they definitely don't play with a lot of subtelty.  They also don't really explore too much of the darker implications of the show and what it means for the outside world.  Overall, a pleasant mass-audience dramedy.  Well-acted, well-paced, fun idea.

Gonna push back on your academy award note though.  The real crime at the Oscars that year for actor in a leading role was not giving it to Edward Norton for American History X.  But that was also the Shakespeare in Love year, so there's a whole other conversation about the shitshow that particular Academy Awards became.
4. I've never really got on with the Truman Show. I've seen it a few times over the years and always thought it was fine, and well-made. But the main problem for me is that it's very frustrating spending a very long time waiting for Truman to realise something that the viewer knows from the first scene. 

I don't feel as though I'm on the journey with him. Instead I'm waiting for him to catch up. And when he finally does catch up, the movie ends. 

I feel like I'm looking down on all the characters, not just Truman. It's clever and original, but it doesn't work for me emotionally.


  - I think there's good evidence for the reading that Truman knows a lot earlier than he lets on. 


You know the scene toward the very beginning of the film when he is gardening with his butt up in the air taking most of the frame?


That's the exact spot where his secret tunnel exits for his escape. 


I think most of the movie is him testing his loved ones to determine if they're all in on it. 


So I think he knows and has known since the encounter with the off-script extra, but he doesn't want to accept the truth about his wife and best friend. Watching it with the idea that he knows but can't let anyone know he knows while he interrogates his loved ones turns the tension and heartbreak up for me.
5. At the time, as reality TV changed television forever, it was a thought provoking and I loved the concept. I had read the screenplay which was set in Manhattan, which gave it amazing scale. Maybe it was my expectations but the scaled down setting of the movie took a lot of the magic away. I recently rewatched and as much as I appreciate Jim Carey as an actor, I think he was still transitioning from his broad comedies at this time and his performance is weird and stilted. Why does he act like he’s in a sitcom, when he doesn’t know he’s on TV. Noah Emmerich gives a great performance. But overall I felt it was disappointing. 
  - He acts like he’s in a sitcom because he was born and raised in a sitcom world. They created that character world around him and shaped him to be a sitcom character. 

## 3. Honestly...didn't find The Bride! a "mess" at all - not sure what people's problem was

**Score:** 10
**Body:** Honestly, I'd take this adventuresome Frankenstein story over Del Toro's Frankenstein all day long.

Bale and Buckley gave great performances in somewhat difficult roles (easy to hit the wrong notes on), I think one of Bale's most enjoyable performances in a while and I'm a fan. The performances alone give this film a recommendation The cinematography was absolutely beautiful, the color palettes of scenes and lighting were so on point, rich and expressive. It had interesting, subtle things to say about the role of film and icons in film, how we project ourselves onto screens, and screens project themselves into us and our identities, and perhaps even more interesting things about being possessed by authors of literature, and intensive authors of the past, Mary Shelley able to reach from the grave into all of us. Some really memorably scenes (the big dance scene I loved), and presences like Jake Gyllenhaal. It was uneven some in tone, the shifting into new set ups that felt a bit like new movies (surprise, its a movie celebrating "movies"!), but really not all that jarring once you caught on. The whole Penelope Cruz and Sarsgaard thing, I haven't a clue what was happening there and why it got so much screen time. I mean, I "get" the messages but it ultimately really felt like a distraction. I would have much preferred more of Mary Shelly penetrating The Bride! reality and wrestling for control throughout instead of mostly disappearing, except for wordplay (I think the script missed a turn there). But honestly, given all the negative hullabaloo I was pretty surprised how structurally mild the film was, I expected some sort of cacophony of filmmaking.

The only thing is, and it was a big one, as much as I had read that this was some sort of feminist anthem (and yes, full of feminist ideas and tropes of resistance - did love the ink-stained mouth - Romantic tuberculosis cough up - and fingers, ink as blood for Mary) the main character really wasn't "free" or even breaking away with disordered "geometry" much at all. In fact she kind of was that sort before her first death. She mostly was the "sidekick" of Frankenstein, the bad girl with the bad boy boyfriend, Nancy with her "Sid", and even in her chosen name she is positioned towards an implied, if virtual "Groom", and matrimony itself (even if she prefers not to marry anyone). It felt somewhat "light" in the radicalness of her presented breakaway, caught in film cliches of bad girl cinema and being defined opposite the more bankable powerful male "(co)lead". There was much, much more of Bale in this than I imagined would be, not just in terms of screen time, but also how much he was the the gravity which swung a lot of plot development.

On the other hand, letting the question of feminism go, it did feel like a beautiful film about trauma, with death and revitalization about strategies for living post-trauma, with all of the in-habitation of film and literature that can help after trauma, and a sort of vitalistic co-dependency wherein both can give each other reasons for living. The answer to trauma found in Bale's line "It's fucking terrible I know. There is nothing left to do now but live."

Tons of cinema references and quotebacks, literature as well, some obvious (can't breathe in the Bell Jar), some subtle (drive-in movie scene, maybe suggesting White Heat?). Visually so beautiful. Banging acting filling the screen, inventive stylizations. What's not to love?

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I admire what Gyllenhaal was going for her with some of her ideas (re-telling from the Bride's perspective becoming an excuse to bring up some heavy topics/giving us the rom-com that '35 film never did; for it ended right when the two monsters meet) but she couldn't stick the landing. Like you have such a turn-off scene of the bride being assaulted THIS TIME in a slower pace by a police officer - like it was straight out of Crash 2005... to prove a point I guess, but when Bale and Buckley were acting this fantastical all through the movie, and really minutes after a musical number (that went nowehere btw) you're throwing us this? to what to make a point?

This kind of "change of tone" which has become so fashionable in a post-Tarantino cinema may look easy but requires unspeakable skill, a certain touch... which I think Maggie lacks, that's why her movie comes off as loud and messy rather than focusd and controlled (yes, even "messy" narratives can be more cohesive than this). Seems to me she brought her ideas to the screen half-heartedly: part of her focus was to make something (partly)fun with some of her favorite people in her life (they're all family and friends) and in the end, my enjoyment of the movie comes down more to her leads' work rathern than a satisfying story and style on its own.
  - >Like you have such a turn-off scene of the bride being assaulted THIS TIME in a slower pace by a police officer

For me, the deeper point of that scene was that she was actually being raped by (via) poetry, as a woman who held Mary Shelly and the love of language within her. She was being violated at the core of her means of survival, the very thing that pumped through her heart (because her blood was literal ink). 
  - I don’t actually think the issue is “tone” so much as what you’re willing to let sit next to what. The slow assault by the cop felt less like the film refusing to let the rom com fantasy fully overwrite the reality of the world she’s in. This is a world where the same institutions that are supposed to “protect” her (police, media, doctors) are participating in her demise and trying to force a label on her; that scene is part of the fabric instead of some random shock insert to “make a point.” 

And honestly, that whiplash is just reality for a lot of women: one moment you’re dancing with your friends and the next there’s a pair of hands on you, kneading you like you’re a piece of dough. The big musical number and the assault landing close together is exactly the whiplash the Bride lives in: she’s allowed a moment of hyper stylized fantasy and then yanked right back into no violence that structures her environment. This is just the movie insisting that you can’t have the fun, meta, self‑referential Frankenstein rom‑com without also looking at the systems that make a woman like her possible in the first place. You can say Maggie doesn’t pull it off for YOU, but “she just wanted to have fun with her friends” feels like an easy way to dismiss a film that’s actually trying to juggle more than just vibes.
2. It’s a mess in the sense that it doesn’t really commit to anything. The Monster and his Bride reimagined as Sid and Nancy is a fresh idea, but then what? There’s hints of a movie about her starting a feminist revolution, yet it goes nowhere and we never feel that righteousness. There’s a musical sequence, just because. It keeps the viewer at an apathetic distance. 
  - >There’s a musical sequence, just because.

Oh wow, didn't really see it that way. It was a huge release for Bale's character. He constantly fantasized/projected himself onto the screen in films, imagined himself redeemed (from his monstrous self) through Jake's character...but, as he said, he "doesn't dance". He somewhat cripple-ishly "dances" for a moment when he presents the umbrella to The Bride when they encounter...but that is all. ONLY when he is publicly humiliated by his idol (an idol that has held him together this whole time, even a newspaper clipping being enough to calm him) that he finally cracks...and in that breakdown suddenly dance forces its way into him. And the intensity of the dance (invoking the Thriller music video, and Rocky Horror Picture Show's Time Warp - The Bride has a bit of Magenta in her) is so strong that people are possessed by it, this unison of expression. The film is about possession, and the dance scene is maybe the most important possession scene of the film (other than Mary Shelly cracking in).

I think it's commitment is really a story about post-trauma survival and the celebration of film and literature in the service of that.

  - Yeah, I was just so ready to be like: "Okay sure it's a mess, but there is this inner core thing that makes it all make sense" but didn't find it a mess at all. The main pairing was so strong I didn't mind that much. 

A lot of the film came together for me when she does the marlene dietrich impersonation in the railway car, practically becoming possessed by her, just as she was possessed by Mary Shelly, along with Frank's various projections and fantasies in film. It's a film about possession, and in a sense the film itself is alternately "possessed" by other films in cinema history, to varying degrees. Speaking through characters and scenes, to varying degrees.
